text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025

Dr Priya Menon
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Priya Menon,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Epilepsy with learning disability; history of falls without injury; vitamin D insufficiency under GP monitoring; resides in supported living with carer assistance for communication and seizure logging; ex-smoker.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. Lamotrigine 50 mg twice daily. Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily. Omeprazole 20 mg daily. Simvastatin 20 mg at night. Emollient cream as required.

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker, Alex, who helps him with communication and maintains his seizure record. Bill was comfortable, engaged with simple questions, and used short phrases; Alex supplemented details using the home seizure diary and a smartphone log.

Seizures: According to the carer-maintained diary over the past six months, Bill has a focal onset seizure monthly. Events are brief (typically under two minutes), with a consistent semiology of behavioural arrest, staring, lip-smacking, and right hand fumbling, followed by a few minutes of confusion without generalisation or injury. No nocturnal events have been witnessed, and there is no clear trigger identified. Adherence to medication is reported as good, supported by blister packs and staff prompts. There have been no emergency department attendances and no use of rescue medication.

General health and function: Appetite stable, weight unchanged. Sleep routine regular with carer support. No new focal neurological symptoms reported. He attends a weekday day centre and short walks are encouraged by staff; no recent falls. Bowel habits regular. Mood appears settled per carer report.

Examination: Afebrile, BP 128/76, pulse 68 regular. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. Neurological examination: alert, oriented to person and place with mild expressive language difficulty; cranial nerves intact; tone and power normal in all limbs; no tremor or ataxia; gait steady with normal base.

Investigations: Last serum levels and routine bloods checked by the GP in July 2025—U&Es, LFTs, FBC within normal limits; vitamin D adequate on supplementation. No interval neuroimaging since prior clinic, not indicated by current course.

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure medications without change, given stable pattern of a focal onset seizure monthly and good tolerability.
- Reinforce seizure logging via the carer-maintained diary and encourage prompt documentation of any change in duration, frequency, or features.
- Safety: continue supported living measures already in place; no restrictions beyond current care plan.
- Liaise with GP to repeat routine monitoring (U&Es/LFTs/FBC/vitamin D) in 6–9 months as per shared care.
- Provide the team with an updated seizure action summary for the care file.
- Review in 9 months, or sooner if seizure frequency increases or new features emerge.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: has a focal onset seizure monthly

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "According to the carer-maintained diary over the past six months, Bill has a focal onset seizure monthly." This indicates a frequency of 1 seizure per month over the recent 6-month period. No other seizure types or additional frequencies are reported. Therefore, select the highest (and only) frequency: 1 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: has a focal onset seizure monthly
